info.topictitle: B3.4.4 Resistors and capacitors in series & parallel
info.parenttitle: B3.4 Electronic systems application and selection (HL only)

markdown:
## Resistors and Capacitors in Series and Parallel Circuits


<definition term="Series Circuit">A **circuit where components are connected one after another in a single loop**, so **current flows through each component in turn**.</definition>

<definition term="Parallel Circuit">A **circuit where the electrical path splits into branches**, so **current divides** and **only part flows through each branch**.</definition>

![Resistors in series.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3e6954b1cae423a98914e0d2c6e9252f27fe81e9-1908x708.png)

![Resistors in parallel.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/19241e19a44eef33894bbd646e1327422871b73d-2082x1311.png)

### Resistors in Series and Parallel


<definition term="Resistor">An electronic component that **limits or controls the flow of electric current** in a circuit.</definition>

| Configuration | Rule | Example Calculation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Series | Total Resistance (Rₜ) = R₁ + R₂ + R₃… | Rₜ = 10 + 20 = 30 Ω |
| Parallel | 1/Rₜ = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃… | 1/Rₜ = 1/10 + 1/20 → Rₜ = 6.67 Ω |

<callout type="example">* If you have three resistors with values of 2Ω, 3Ω, and 5Ω in **series**, the total resistance is 10Ω.
* For two resistors of 4Ω and 6Ω in **parallel**, the total resistance is 2.4Ω.</callout>

<callout type="tip">* Use **series** to increase resistance.
* Use **parallel** to reduce resistance and allow more current flow.</callout>

### Capacitors in Series and Parallel


<definition term="Capacitor">An electronic component that **stores and releases electrical energy** by holding an **electric charge** between two conductive plates.</definition>

| Configuration | Rule | Example Calculation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Series | 1/Cₜ = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + 1/C₃… | 1/Cₜ = 1/4 + 1/4 → Cₜ = 2 µF |
| Parallel | Total Capacitance (Cₜ) = C₁ + C₂ + C₃… | Cₜ = 4 + 4 = 8 µF |

<callout type="example">* If you have two capacitors of 4μF and 6μF in **series**, the total capacitance is 2.4μF.
* For three capacitors of 2μF, 3μF, and 5μF in **parallel**, the total capacitance is 10μF.</callout>

<callout type="tip">* Use **parallel** to increase storage capacity (e.g., energy backup).
* Use **series** for higher voltage applications.</callout>

### Why This Matters


  1. **Series resistors**: Useful for limiting current (e.g., LED protection).
  2. **Parallel resistors**: Useful for maintaining current across multiple branches.
  3. **Parallel capacitors**: Store more charge => good for power supply smoothing.
  4. **Series capacitors**: Handle higher voltages=> used in filtering and timing.

<callout type="exam_technique">Use correct **units**:

* Resistance (Ω)
* Capacitance (µF or farads)</callout>
